Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty unconventional but it worked for me. I'd dedicate solid blocks of time on weekends, maybe 19 hours across the week, but I wasn't logging into the LMS constantly. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often record lectures or listen to podcasts related to the material while commuting or at the gym. My routine was more about absorbing content through listening than sitting in front of a screen clicking through modules.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on what else was going on in my life. I have extracurriculars that take up significant time, so I'd bunch my studying around those commitments. When deadlines approached, I'd intensify my focus, but otherwise I maintained a loose weekly rhythm. I found that flexibility actually reduced my stress rather than adding to it.
Q3: I'd usually log in when I needed to check specific deadlines or download materials I couldn't access elsewhere. Sometimes it was to see if there were any announcements or changes to the schedule. To be honest, I didn't feel the need to browse around the LMS much since I had my own system for organizing what I needed to study. It was more of a utility than a daily habit.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine the way other tools did. I'd check it periodically, almost like checking mail, but it wasn't something I relied on daily. My routine was built around downloaded materials, audio recordings, and discussions with peers outside the platform.
Q5: The video content was genuinely useful because I could download it and listen to it repeatedly. I watched over 160 minutes of video content, and I'd often convert those to audio or re-listen to key sections. The discussion forums were occasionally helpful when peers raised questions I hadn't considered. Anything I could consume auditorily or discuss with others felt most aligned with how I learn.
Q6: I largely ignored the gamified tasks and some of the interactive modules that felt more like busywork than genuine learning. Reading-heavy resources without audio alternatives often got delayed because they didn't match my learning style. I also didn't engage much with resources that seemed redundant to what I was already covering through my own methods.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly contributed to my understanding or exam preparation. If I could see a clear connection between the task and the learning outcomes, I'd engage with it fully. Activities that felt like they were designed just to generate engagement metrics rather than actual learning didn't appeal to me at all.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they had lower stakes, which sometimes made me skip them entirely unless they seemed genuinely enriching. Required activities got my attention because they were tied to grades, but I'd still evaluate whether they were the most efficient use of my time. The gamified tasks were optional and interesting conceptually, but I often prioritized other methods that I knew worked better for me.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened outside the LMS. I'd download lecture recordings and convert them to audio files for my commute. I used textbooks extensively and had study groups with peers where we'd discuss concepts verbally. I also found podcasts and YouTube lectures from other universities that explained concepts in ways that clicked better for me as an auditory learner.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was deeply engaged with the material but my LMS activity was minimal. I'd be listening to downloaded lectures while exercising, discussing concepts with classmates over coffee, or working through problems on paper. None of that shows up in any log, but it was where the real learning happened for me.
Q11: Yes, this was my standard approach. I'd access a video or document once, download it, and then work with it offline for days or even weeks. The LMS would only show that single access, but I might have reviewed that material ten times. This is especially true for lecture recordings that I'd listen to multiple times while doing other activities.
Q12: Mid-semester got challenging when my extracurricular commitments peaked and I had multiple deadlines converging. Even though my stress was high, my anxiety stayed manageable because I trusted my system. The hardest moments were when I felt like the LMS activities weren't aligned with what would actually help me succeed on exams.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected my LMS usage. When I was busy with extracurriculars, I'd minimize my time on the platform and focus on the most efficient study methods I knew. Fatigue sometimes meant I'd choose passive listening over active LMS engagement. But I always found ways to keep learning even when I couldn't be online.
Q14: There were times I'd log in and scroll through materials without really absorbing anything, usually when I was tired or distracted. I might watch a video but realize halfway through that I wasn't actually processing it. In those moments, I'd usually stop and come back later with fresh ears, so to speak.
Q15: I postponed some assignments when I calculated that my time was better spent elsewhere. With a 29% assignment completion rate, I clearly made strategic choices about what to prioritize. I knew exams were weighted heavily, so I focused my energy there. It wasn't avoidance out of laziness but a deliberate allocation of limited time.
Q16: Deadlines created spikes in my LMS activity. I'd log in more frequently as due dates approached to double-check requirements and submit work. But between deadlines, my LMS presence dropped significantly because I was studying through other means. The platform became a checkpoint system rather than a continuous learning environment.
Q17: Feedback definitely influenced my approach. When I received positive feedback on exam performance, it reinforced that my methods were working despite low LMS engagement. Constructive feedback on assignments helped me understand what professors valued. I'd adjust my studying based on that rather than changing my LMS habits.
Q18: The course structure gave me enough flexibility to succeed my own way. Having materials available for download was crucial for my learning style. The weighting toward exams over continuous assessment actually suited me well since I could demonstrate my knowledge in concentrated bursts rather than through constant online activity.
Q19: The availability of video content made engagement easier because I could adapt it to my auditory learning style. What made it harder was when important information was only available through text-based modules that required staying logged in. The gamified elements were a nice touch but didn't fundamentally change how I approached the course.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding but struggling to find it in the materials provided. They could be clicking through content trying to make sense of things without being able to translate that into completed work. Or they might be dealing with personal issues that fragment their attention even when they're technically present on the platform.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My LMS activity was low, but I earned a distinction because my actual learning was happening elsewhere. The platform captured maybe 20% of my real engagement with the course material. Students like me have developed effective learning strategies that simply don't require constant LMS presence.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accesses materials and submits work, which is useful for identifying students who might be completely disengaged. But they miss the depth of engagement, the quality of understanding, and all the learning that happens offline. They can't capture conversations with peers, independent reading, or the mental work of synthesizing information.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense and connects to what I already know. It's about curiosity and the drive to understand, not just completing tasks. For me, engagement looks like listening to a lecture three times, discussing it with friends, and then being able to explain it in my own words. It's internal and active, not necessarily visible.
Q24: The LMS data would completely miss how much I learned through listening and discussion. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with audio materials during my commute or the peer study sessions that solidified my understanding. It also wouldn't capture my high motivation and the strategic decisions I made about where to invest my time. My distinction grade tells a story that my login frequency never could.
